---
title: "1. Plan & Design"
description: "Explore existing resources, check legal requirements, write data management plans, design studies, and prepare preregistrations"
body-class: orc-phase-1
phase_order: 1
sidebar: open-research-cycle
number-depth: 4
number-sections: true
number-offset: [0,1,0]
filters: 
    - assets/remove_anchor.lua
page-layout: full
css: orc-styles.css
title-block-style: none   
include-in-header:
  - text: |
      <link href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.0/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css" rel="stylesheet">
---

<div class="orc-phase-hero-compact phase-1">
<div class="orc-hero-cycle phase-1">
{{< include ../images/os-cycle.svg >}}
</div>
<div class="orc-hero-body">
<div class="orc-hero-tagline">Set the foundation for open & reliable research</div>
<div class="orc-hero-pills phase-1">
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Explore & Reuse</span> 
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Check Legal Frameworks</span>
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Write Data Management Plan</span>
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Design Study</span>
</div>
<div class="orc-hero-checkpoint phase-1"><i class="bi bi-people-fill"></i> Checkpoints: Study Plan Presentation & Preregistration Submission</div>
</div>
</div>


<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary><i class="bi bi-search"></i>

### Explore and Reuse {#sec-explore-and-reuse}

</summary> 

Any resource that inspire you or that you want to reuse and/or adapt must minimally be **cited using their DOIs** (and otherwise URL with author, date, and time of access) and you must **follow the license and/or usage agreement** provided by the authors. 
A research output (e.g. data, code) without a license or statement granting your permission for reuse cannot legally be reused even if they appear publicly online.

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### 1.1.1. Articles  <!--make this, and other tabs title everywhere, a shareable url, cross referencable within this website  -->

* **Review existing literature** on open source registries like [OpenAlex](https://openalex.org/) to come up with a well-founded research question,
* **Use a reference manager** to keep track of your bibliography. [Zotero](https://www.zotero.org/) is an open source software that can be integrated within RStudio to write reproducible manuscripts seamlessly, formatting your bibliography in any format you want (see [3. Analyze & Collaborate](03_analyze_and_collaborate.qmd){.mark}).

<!-- need to include cards and text for Open Alex PuMed -> Europe PMC and preprint servers -->

<!-- Preprint Servers

Share manuscripts before peer review to get early feedback and establish priority:

::: {.orc-grid-tools}

<div class="orc-card-tool">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><img src="https://cdn.simpleicons.org/osf" alt="OSF"></div>
<h4>[OSF Preprints](https://osf.io/preprints/)</h4>
<p>Multidisciplinary preprint service</p>
<details class="orc-card-details">
<summary>Read more</summary>
<p>All disciplines welcome. Integration with OSF projects, flexible requirements.</p>
</details>
</div>

<div class="orc-card-tool">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><i class="bi bi-file-earmark-medical"></i></div>
<h4>[bioRxiv](https://www.biorxiv.org/)</h4>
<p>Biological sciences preprints</p>
<details class="orc-card-details">
<summary>Read more</summary>
<p>Free hosting, DOI assignment, rapid posting. Widely recognized in life sciences and neuroscience.</p>
</details>
</div>

<div class="orc-card-tool">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><i class="bi bi-heart-pulse"></i></div>
<h4>[medRxiv](https://www.medrxiv.org/)</h4>
<p>Medical and health sciences preprints</p>
<details class="orc-card-details">
<summary>Read more</summary>
<p>Medical research focus with health screening. DOI assignment, linked to bioRxiv infrastructure.</p>
</details>
</div>

<div class="orc-card-tool">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><i class="bi bi-calculator"></i></div>
<h4>[arXiv](https://arxiv.org/)</h4>
<p>Physics, mathematics, computer science</p>
<details class="orc-card-details">
<summary>Read more</summary>
<p>Established 1991. Moderation system, LaTeX support. Standard in quantitative fields.</p>
</details>
</div>

::: -->



<!-- 
This section uses Bootstrap structure to create a responsive card layout.
- The main .container div must always be present.
- Each section (Learn More / Tools & Resources) is wrapped in a .row.
- IF one of the section is not needed, remove the entire .row block and its downstream .col divs.

HOW TO ADD CARDS?
- Each card should always be placed inside a .col div.
- To add a new card, simply copy an existing .col block and edit the links, img, title, and text.
- Add as many .col blocks as needed within the .row, the cards are automatically laid out 3 cards per row and any additional cards will wrap to the next row.

-->
<!-- main container div -->
::: {.container .my-4 }

<h4 class="fs-5 no-anchor"><i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE</h4>

<!-- row for Learn More section -->
::: {.row .row-cols-1 .row-cols-md-2 .row-cols-lg-3 .g-4}

<!-- 1st card -->
::: {.col}
<!-- Actual card component -->
<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-2">
<img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/95/Zotero_icon.png" class="img-fluid" alt="Zotero logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Introduction to Zotero</h4>
<p class="card-text">Use an open source reference manager. (1h)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/introduction-to-zotero/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"> </a>
</div>

:::
<!-- Add more cards if needed after this block -->
:::


<h4 class="fs-5 mt-3"><i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS & RESOURCES</h4>
<!-- row for Tools & Resources section -->

::: {.row .row-cols-1 .row-cols-md-2 .row-cols-lg-3 .g-4}

<!-- 1st card -->
::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2"> 
<img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/OpenAlex_2025_favicon.svg/250px-OpenAlex_2025_favicon.svg.png" class="img-fluid" alt="OpenAlex icon">
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">OpenAlex</h4>
<p class="card-text"> All the world's research, connected and open. </p>
<a href="https://openalex.org/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>

</div>

:::

:::

:::

#### 1.1.2. Preregistrations

A **preregistration** typically consists of a hypothesis and predictions, a plan for data collection (when relevant), and a plan for data analysis, that researchers upload before starting their projects, often in order to increase the rigor of confirmatory research (see [1.4.1. Pre-analysis planning](<!--internal link-->)). <!-- this link doesnot work-->
<!-- this cross ref is not working, it's not a md title FIXME either link to the study design ana analysis plan title or the tab pre analysis plan if possible. with an internal looking cross ref greyish thing-->

<br>

To have some **insight into projects that are not (yet) published**, either currently ongoing or abandoned, you can therefore look for projects that were preregistered. Projects that are left unpublished typically have a note attached to their preregistration. Some registries are discipline-specific while others are discipline-agnostic.

::: {.container .my-4 }

<h4 class="fs-5 mt-3"><i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS & RESOURCES</h4>

::: {.row .row-cols-1 .row-cols-md-2 .row-cols-lg-3 .g-4}

::: {.col}
<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2"> 
<img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg/3840px-Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg.png" class="img-fluid" alt="OSF icon">
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Open Science Framework</h4>
<p class="card-text">Registry of preregistrations. Widely used across fields.</p>
<a href="https://osf.io/registries/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>

</div>

:::

::: {.col}
<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2"> 
<img src="https://aspredicted.org/favicon.ico" class="img-fluid" alt="AsPredicted icon">
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">AsPredicted</h4>
<p class="card-text">Registry of simple preregistrations</p>
<a href="https://aspredicted.org/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>

</div>

:::

::: {.col}
<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2"> 
<img src="https://preclinicaltrials.eu/images/logo-pct.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="PreclinicalTrials.eu icon">
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">PreclinicalTrials.eu</h4>
<p class="card-text">Registry of preclinical animal study protocols.</p>
<a href="https://preclinicaltrials.eu/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>

</div>

:::

::: {.col}
<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2"> 
<img src="https://www.animalstudyregistry.org/asr_web/image/asr_logo.png" class="img-fluid" alt="animalstudyregistry.org icon">
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">AnimalStudyRegistry.org</h4>
<p class="card-text">Registry of animal studies.</p>
<a href="https://www.animalstudyregistry.org/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>

</div>

:::

::: {.col}
<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2"> 
<img src="https://clinicaltrials.gov/assets/favicons/favicon.svg" class="img-fluid" alt="ClinicalTrials.gov icon">
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">ClinicalTrials.gov</h4>
<p class="card-text">Registry of clinical trial protocols.</p>
<a href="https://clinicaltrials.gov/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>

</div>

:::

:::

:::


#### 1.1.3. Data

To find **existing datasets**, search for discipline-specific repositories on [re3data](https://www.re3data.org/) which is a central registry of all repositories, or explore subject agnostic repositories such as [DataCite](https://datacite.org/), [FigShare](https://figshare.com/browse), [Open Science Framework (OSF)](https://osf.io/search), or [Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/) (oftentimes with the corresponding analysis code). These platforms either give you access to existing data or provide _metadata_ and explanations on how to request access to the data. **Metadata** are data about your data, such as author, date, measurement device, unit of measurement, context of data collection, ect.

<br>

Found a dataset to reuse? 

<br>

* **Review metadata and documentation.** Make sure you know where your data comes from, how the data was collected and processed, and reflect on whether any of it poses problems for your research question. 
* **Check what requirements the data sources have.** Some requirement to access the data may be to submit a preregistration (see [1.4 Study Design & Analysis Plan](#sec-study-design-analysis-plan){.mark}).
* **Use the metadata to plan your analysis.** A data dictionary (or "codebook") and other documentation describing the variables, range of values, ect., may be available and should be reviewed. To minimize confirmation or hindsight bias, do not plot the data immediately; instead, prepare a pre-analysis plan by examining the documentation.(see [1.4 Study Design & Analysis Plan](#sec-study-design-analysis-plan){.mark}).  


::: {.container .my-4 }

<h4 class="fs-5 mt-3"><i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS & RESOURCES</h4>

::: {.row .row-cols-1 .row-cols-md-2 .row-cols-lg-3 .g-4}

<!--1st card-->
::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2"> 
<img src="https://www.re3data.org/favicon.ico" class="img-fluid" alt="re3data icon">
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">re3data</h4>
<p class="card-text">Registry of research data repositories.</p>
<a href="https://www.re3data.org/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>

</div>

:::

<!--2nd card-->
::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2"> 
<img src="https://datacite.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/DataCite-Logo_Schwoop.svg" class="img-fluid" alt="DataCite icon">
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">DataCite Commons</h4>
<p class="card-text">Discovery tool connecting works, people, and organizations.</p>
<a href="https://commons.datacite.org/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>

</div>

:::

<!-- card-->
::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2"> 
<img src="https://websitev3-p-eu.figstatic.com/assets-v3/97a584d494444650f01c8ba85b077c4f7938fbce/static/media/favicon-70.66d0d88a.png" class="img-fluid" alt="FigShare icon">
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Fig Share</h4>
<p class="card-text">General-purpose repository for data, software, reports.</p>
<a href="https://figshare.com/browse" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>

</div>

:::

::: {.col}
<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2"> 
<img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg/3840px-Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg.png" class="img-fluid" alt="OSF icon">
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Open Science Framework</h4>
<p class="card-text">General-purpose repository for data, materials, reports.</p>
<a href="https://osf.io/registries/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>

</div>

:::


<!-- card-->
::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2"> 
<img src="https://about.zenodo.org/static/img/icons/zenodo-icon-blue.svg" class="img-fluid" alt="Zenodo icon">
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Zenodo</h4>
<p class="card-text">General-purpose repository for data, software, reports.</p>
<a href="https://zenodo.org" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>

</div>

:::

:::

:::


#### 1.1.4. Code

You may find **code available for reuse** archived on [Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/) or actively developed on [GitHub](https://github.com/). Learn how to work with GitHub in more details in [3. Analyze & Collaborate](03_analyze_and_collaborate.qmd){.mark} or start learning Git version control now! 
 
<br>

:::{.callout-important}
Code publicly visible on GitHub without a license or equivalent text explicitly stating permission for reuse cannot be legally reused. The best is to ask the authors to add an **open license** to their repository to allow reuse (for this, they can for instance add a file called LICENSE.txt with e.g. the [Apache 2.0 license text](https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0.txt) that allows reuse - see our [code publishing tutorial](https://lmu-osc.github.io/code-publishing/choose_license.html) to learn more about licenses).
:::


::: {.container .my-4 }

<h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE</h4>

<!-- row for Learn More section -->
::: {.row .row-cols-1 .row-cols-md-2 .row-cols-lg-3 .g-4}

<!-- 1st card -->
::: {.col}
<!-- Actual card component -->
<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-2">
<img src="https://www.svgrepo.com/show/303548/git-icon-logo.svg" class="img-fluid" alt="Git logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Git Tutorial</h4>
<p class="card-text">Use version control system Git from within RStudio. (2h)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/Introduction-RStudio-Git-GitHub/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"> </a>
</div>

:::

<!-- 2nd card -->
::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-2">
<img src="https://cdn.simpleicons.org/github" class="img-fluid" alt="GitHub logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">GitHub Tutorial</h4>
<p class="card-text">Collaborative coding with GitHub and RStudio (1h)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/Collaborative-RStudio-GitHub/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"> </a>
</div>

:::

:::

<h4 class="fs-5 mt-3"><i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS & RESOURCES</h4>
<!-- row for Tools & Resources section -->

::: {.row .row-cols-1 .row-cols-md-2 .row-cols-lg-3 .g-4}

<!-- 1st card -->
::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2"> 
<img src="https://about.zenodo.org/static/img/icons/zenodo-icon-blue.svg" class="img-fluid" alt="Zenodo icon">
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Zenodo</h4>
<p class="card-text">General-purpose repository for data, software, reports.</p>
<a href="https://zenodo.org/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>

</div>

:::

<!-- 2nd card -->
::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2"> 
<img src="https://cdn.simpleicons.org/github" class="img-fluid" alt="GitHub icon">
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">GitHub</h4>
<p class="card-text">Cloud-based platform to collaborate on code.</p>
<a href="https://github.com/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>

</div>

:::

:::

:::


</details>


<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary><i class="bi bi-shield-check"></i>

### Legal Requirements {#sec-legal-requirements}

</summary>

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### 1.2.1. LMU guidelines

The [LMU Guidelines for Safeguarding Good Scientific Practice](https://cms-cdn.lmu.de/media/contenthub/amtliche-veroeffentlichungen/gwp-ordnung.pdf) (in german) are legally binding for all academics, researchers, research support staff, teachers, and students at LMU Munich. Only the original text in German prevails, but here is an English summary of relevant aspects for this guide: 

<br>

<h5>Appropriate level of documentation and standards to allow **reproduction**:</h5> <!-- individually remove anchor tags for these titles within pages -->

* Reproducible methods must be used. (§11)
* When research software is developed, its source code must be documented. (§12)

<h5>Appropriate level of documentation and standards to allow **replication**:</h5>

* All information relevant to the production of a research result must be documented comprehensively to enable replication. (§7 and §12)
* If specific professional recommendations exist for review and evaluation, the results must be documented in accordance with these respective specifications. (§12) 
* Individual results that do not support the hypothesis must also be documented; **a selection of results is not permitted**. (§12)

<h5>Public access to research results:</h5>

* Apart from specific exceptions, **all findings should be made public**. For this, they must be described in a detailed and comprehensible manner which includes making available the research data, materials and information on which the results are based, as well as the methods used and the software employed (including appropriately licensed self-written software) **according to the [FAIR principles]{.underline}**. (§13)
* Data, material, software made publicly accessible must be appropriately archived, usually for a period of 10 years (§17).

<br>

In later sections, you will acquire skills in FAIR data management and reproducible workflow that will enable you to comply with these guidelines.

<br>

:::{.callout-note}

The **[FAIR principles]{.underline}** are defined as:  

- **F**indable: metadata should be deposited in a searchable repository and be assigned a permanent identifier\
- **A**ccessible: the data is either open, or accessible upon some authentication process, or closed, but with open metadata.\
- **I**nteroperable: the data is described with a standard terminology (so the dataset can be merged with other ones) and saved in a stable file format\
- **R**eusable: the data is richly documented (e.g. with a data dictionary) and is accompanied by a data usage license

**Metadata** are data about your data, such as author, date, measurement device, unit of measurement, context of data collection, ect.

<br>

See [https://www.go-fair.org/fair-principles/](https://www.go-fair.org/fair-principles/) for more information and section [2. Collect & Manage](02_collect_and_manage.qmd){.mark} to learn how to implement the FAIR principles in your research.
:::

::: {.container .my-4}

<h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS & RESOURCES</h4>

::: {.row .row-cols-1 .row-cols-md-2 .row-cols-lg-3 .g-4}

::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool w-100 h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2">
<img src="https://cms-cdn.lmu.de/assets/img/favicon.ico" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU logo">
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">LMU Guidelines for Safeguarding Good Scientific Practice</h4>
<p class="card-text">Implementation of the German Research Foundation's (DFG) Code of Conduct</p>
<a href="https://cms-cdn.lmu.de/media/contenthub/amtliche-veroeffentlichungen/gwp-ordnung.pdf" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>
</div>

:::

:::

:::

#### 1.2.2. Funders

Funders may have additional **open and reproducible science requirements** on top of those indicated in the LMU guidelines. For instance, some calls request a Research Data Management plan before making their second payment, some specify the extent and timing of data sharing and provide funds for such activity. Check all requirements in the call information sheets.

<br>

**Contact the [LMU Research Funding Unit](https://www.lmu.de/en/research/research-services/contact.html) to review your grant proposal** and assess if your proposal is meeting your funders' open science requirements. 

#### 1.2.3. Ethics

Data collection and analyses conducted on animals and humans typically requires the approval of your Faculty's ethics committee to make sure your research will be conducted responsibly, and that participants’ data will be protected.

<br>

##### <i class="bi bi-list-check"></i> Your ethics proposal will typically include information on: 

* **Data storage and retention** – outlining how data will be securely stored, backed up, and retained over time. This information can be extracted from a more detailed Research Data Management plan (see @sec-research-data-management).
* **Risks if the data were leaked** – identifying potential consequences for participants or the research project if confidentiality is breached.
* **Data anonymization** – describing procedures to remove or obscure personally identifiable information to protect participant privacy (see [2.3.2. Anonymization](<!--internal link-->) for options, from simple techniques of anonymization to the creation of synthetic data).
* **Language of the informed consent forms** – ensuring that participants clearly understand the purpose, procedures, and any potential risks of the study. Conditions for sharing their data should be clearly explained here (see [2.3.1. Informed Consent](<!--internal link-->)). 
* **Power analysis** to justify sample size – providing a statistical rationale for the number of participants, which supports the validity and ethical justification of the study. This, and more detailed information on the statistical plan, can be extracted from your pre-analysis plan (see [1.4.1. Pre-analysis planning](<!--internal link-->) and [1.4.3. Power analyses](<!--internal link-->)). <!-- wil these links work?? if not, let's not leave dead links all over the place, let's keep them makred with a comment, if needed we can tag the header of the section though leave the text identifying the subsection intact.  those linking panel tabsets dont work-->


For data protection guidance, contact the [LMU Data Protection Officer](https://www.lmu.de/en/about-lmu/structure/central-university-administration/legal-matters/data-protection/) or the [Research Data Management team of the University Library ](https://www.en.ub.uni-muenchen.de/writing/research_data/research-data-management/index.html).

<!-- should be a team SOP with application language, consent form language ect -->


::: {.container .my-4}

<h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE</h4>

::: {.row .row-cols-1 .row-cols-md-2 .row-cols-lg-3 .g-4}


::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool w-100 h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Data Management Plans</h4>
<p class="card-text">Overview of components, tips, and tools. (30 min)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/FAIR-Data-Management/dmp.html" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>
</div>

:::

::: {.col}
<div class="card orc-card-tool w-100 h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial (tba) </span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Data Anonymization</h4>
<p class="card-text">Implement data anonymization techniques in R. (X h)</p>
<!-- Add link when available -->
<a href="#" class="stretched-link" rel="noopener"></a>
</div>

:::

::: {.col}
<div class="card orc-card-tool w-100 h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Power Analysis</h4>
<p class="card-text">Data simulations for GLMs, LMEs, and SEMs in R. (6h)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/Simulations-for-Advanced-Power-Analyses/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener">
</a>
</div>

:::

:::

:::


:::

</details>



<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary><i class="bi bi-folder2-open"></i>

### Research Data Management Plans {#sec-research-data-management}

</summary>

A Data Management Plan (DMP) documents how you will handle research data throughout your project. Writing a DMP prompts you to think and document decisions you might otherwise leave implicit. For instance, you must decide before data collection whether you will eventually share your data publicly and where, in order to get ethics approval on the right plan, design consent form for participants, collect appropriate metadata for the target repository, ect (see lectures "[Why share data openly?](https://lmu-osc.github.io/training/data-management/open-data.html)" and "[Maintaining privacy with open data](https://lmu-osc.github.io/training/data-management/maintaining-privacy-with-open-data.html)").  

##### <i class="bi bi-list-check"></i> Your DMP will ask:

- **What data** will you collect or generate (types, formats, volume, sources)? See also [2.1. Data Collection](02_collect_and_manage.html#sec-data-collection){.mark}.
- **How will you describe it** (metadata standards, documentation practices)? See also [2.2. Data Management](02_collect_and_manage.html#sec-data-management){.mark} for these and the next questions.
- **How will you organize files** (naming conventions, folder structure, versioning)?
- **Where will you store it** (locations, backups, access controls)?
- **How will you ensure quality** (validation checks, error-handling)?
- **How will you share outputs** (repositories, licenses, embargo periods)? See also [4.1. Data Sharing](04_preserve_and_share.html#sec-data-sharing){.mark}
- **What constraints apply** (consent, anonymization, GDPR, data use agreements)? See also [1.2.3. Ethics](){.mark} and [2.3. Ethics & Privacy](02_collect_and_manage.html#sec-ethics-and-privacy){.mark}

The specific questions vary by discipline, data type, and funder requirements. DMP tools like [RDMO](https://rdmo.ub.lmu.de/) guide you through the relevant questions with funder-specific templates. Your DMP is a living document: start with what you know, and refine the details as your project develops (see [2.2. Data Management](02_collect_and_manage.qmd#sec-data-management){.mark}, [3.1. Data Processing & Analysis](03_analyze_and_collaborate.html#sec-data-processing-analysis){.mark}, and [4.1. Data Sharing](04_preserve_and_share.html#sec-data-sharing){.mark}).

<!-- should be a team SOP 
Policies / SOPs support consistent DMP updates (lab-level) that define when a DMP must be updated, who is
responsible, and how updates are reviewed/approved and communicated. -->

::: {.container .my-4}
<h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE</h4>

::: {.row .row-cols-1 .row-cols-md-2 .row-cols-lg-3 .g-4 .mb-4}

::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool w-100 h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Lecture</span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Why share data openly?</h4>
<p class="card-text">An introduction to the what, why, and how to make data open (30 min)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/training/data-management/open-data.html" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>
</div>

:::

::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool w-100 h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Lecture</span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Maintaining Privacy with Open Data</h4>
<p class="card-text">How to make data open without revealing sensitive information (1h)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/training/data-management/maintaining-privacy-with-open-data.html" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>
</div>

:::

::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool w-100 h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Data Management Plans</h4>
<p class="card-text">Overview of components, tips, and tools. (30 min)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/FAIR-Data-Management/dmp.html" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>
</div>

:::

:::

<h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS & RESOURCES</h4>

::: {.row .row-cols-1 .row-cols-md-2 .row-cols-lg-3 .g-4}

::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">Supported at LMU</span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="https://cms-cdn.lmu.de/assets/img/favicon.ico" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU logo">
</div>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-2">
<img src="https://rdmorganiser.github.io/img/logos/RDMO-logo.png" class="img-fluid" alt="RDMO logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">RDMO</h4>
<p class="card-text">Funder-compliant DMP templates (e.g. DFG, ERC).</p>
<a href="https://rdmo.ub.lmu.de/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"> </a>
</div>

:::


::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool w-100 h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2">
<img src="https://riojournal.com/i/ico/rio.ico" class="img-fluid" alt="RIOjournal icon">
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">RIOjournal</h4>
<p class="card-text"> Examples of DMPs by discipline. </p>
<a href="https://riojournal.com/browse_journal_articles.php?form_name=filter_articles&selfurl=&backurl=&sortby=0&journal_id=17" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>
</div>

:::

:::

:::

</details>



<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary><i class="bi bi-clipboard-check"></i>

### Study Design & Analysis Plan {#sec-study-design-analysis-plan}

</summary>

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### 1.4.1. Pre-analysis planning 

##### Why should you plan your statistical plan prior to collecting data? 

Humans are prone to cognitive biases such as **confirmation bias** (seeking information that supports existing beliefs) and **hindsight bias** (believing outcomes were predictable after the fact). In research, these biases can distort findings, especially when researchers make analytic decisions after seeing results. Although statistical testing typically accepts a 5% false positive rate, **"researcher degrees of freedom"** — choices about data collection, exclusions, transformations, sample size, covariates, etc. — can dramatically inflate false positives when decisions are made post hoc. Practices like increasing sample size until reaching statistical significance, selectively removing outliers, or trying multiple analytic strategies **increase the likelihood of false-positive results**. <!-- p-Hacking interactive demo  https://shinyapps.org/apps/p-hacker/ - See how easy it is to find “significant” results -->

<br>

The core problem is that analyses guided by observed outcomes allow biases to influence decisions, making many reported effects unreliable. A key **remedy is transparency and preregistration.**

<br>

##### Benefits of preregistration 

 Preregistration, that is, specifying hypotheses, methods, and analysis plans before data collection or analysis, constrains bias in confirmatory testing while still allowing exploratory analyses, clearly **distinguishing robust hypothesis tests from hypothesis-generating work**. This improves credibility, limits false positives, and often leads to better study design through **early methodological feedback**.

<br>

Preregistration can be beneficial for various type of studies, including: 

- experimental studies (i.e. studies with a manipulated variable): it will define what will be your confirmatory analysis and strengthens your claim
- observational or exploratory studies: it will help you move along the exploratory-confirmatory continuum
- qualitative studies: it will provide a way to document e.g. your positionality towards a subject in the course of a project.

A great tool to create your statistical plan, especially for early career researchers still learning statistics and needing feedback from supervisors, collaborators, or statisticians on their design, is to **simulate data and write the possible statistical test to analyze that data** (see [1.3.2. Simulation of data](<!--internal link-->) and [1.3.3. Power analyses](<!--internal link-->)). 

<br>

##### <i class="bi bi-list-check"></i> What is included in a preregistration?

Several [preregistration templates](https://help.osf.io/article/330-welcome-to-registrations) exist. While the [standard OSF preregistration template](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1gkN0Jp6Gu7GIA4Ne4YCDZ61nCLQRgt32moRdUg9AnVg/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.fwbi14d4b65g) is most commonly used, some are tailored for specific field or specific methods (e.g. systematic review, qualitative work, secondary data analysis).

<br>

Your preregistration will define your study's:
<br>

- **Hypothesis and predictions**
- **Data collection procedures**
- **Sample size and stopping rule**
- **Variables** (manipulated, measured, indices)
- **Statistical method** (model, dependent and independent variables, covariables, transformations)
- **Data exclusion criteria**
- **How to deal with missing data**
<br>

##### Publishing process

Once your study plan is finalized:
<br>

* **Submit your preregistration before collecting or analyzing existing data.** You can do so on discipline specific registries (see [1.1.2. Preregistrations](<!--internal link-->)) or discipline agnostic repositories such as the [Open Science Framework (OSF)](https://osf.io/). 
* **Embargoe your plan if you are concerned about scooping.** On the OSF, your preregistration can be kept private for a predetermine amount of time, and for a maximum of 4 years.
* **Include your pregistration's DOI in your manuscript.** Make your registration public upon the publication of your manuscript. 

This process improves transparency and is inviting valuable **early feedback from collaborators**. An even stronger approach is **submitting preregistrations directly to journals (then called "Registered Reports")**, enabling peer review at a stage where methodological adjustments are still possible.

<br>

##### Registered Reports

Registered Reports are a publication format, now adopted by over 300 journals (see [participating journals](https://www.cos.io/initiatives/registered-reports)), where **preregistrations are peer-reviewed before data collection**. Reviewers evaluate the hypotheses, methods, and planned analyses, allowing methodological improvements. If the plan is approved, the journal grants in-principle acceptance, meaning publication is guaranteed provided researchers follow the protocol.

<br>

After completing the study, authors add results and discussion sections, clearly separating preregistered confirmatory analyses from exploratory ones. Final review focuses on adherence to the approved plan and the validity of conclusions, not on whether results are significant. This model shifts **incentives toward asking important questions and using rigorous methods rather than chasing striking outcomes**.


::: {.container .my-4}

<h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE</h4>

::: {.row .row-cols-1 .row-cols-md-2 .row-cols-lg-3 .g-4 .mb-4}

::: {.col}
<div class="card orc-card-tool w-100 h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial (tba)</span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Preregistration tutorial</h4>
<p class="card-text">Step-by-step guide to creating preregistration. (TBA h)</p>
<a href="#" class="stretched-link"></a>
</div>

:::

:::

<h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS & RESOURCES</h4>

::: {.row .row-cols-1 .row-cols-md-2 .row-cols-lg-3 .g-4}

::: {.col}
<div class="card orc-card-tool w-100 h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2">
<img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg/3840px-Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg.png" alt="COS icon" class="img-fluid">
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Center for Open Science</h4>
<p class="card-text">Preregistration templates, list of journals offering Registered Reports.</p>
<a href="https://www.cos.io/initiatives/registered-reports" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener">
</a>
</div>

:::

::: {.col}
<div class="card orc-card-tool w-100 h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2">
<img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg/3840px-Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg.png" alt="OSF icon" class="img-fluid">
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Open Science Framework</h4>
<p class="card-text">
Preregistration templates, embargoes, file storage.
</p>
<a href="https://help.osf.io/article/330-welcome-to-registrations" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>
</div>

:::

:::

:::

#### 1.4.2. Simulation of Data

In our context, a computer simulation is the generation of artificial data to build up an understanding of real data and the statistical models we use to analyze them. You can simulate data to:

<br>

- **Test your statistical intuition or demonstrate mathematical properties you cannot easily anticipate**.  
_Example: Check whether there are more than 5% significant effects for a variable in a model when supposedly random data are generated._

- **Understand sampling theory and probability distributions or test whether you understand the underlying processes of your system.**  
_Example: See whether simulated data drawn from specific distributions is comparable to real data._

- **Perform power analyses.**  
_Example: Assess whether the sample size (within a simulation repetition) is high enough to detect a simulated effect in more than 80% of the cases. (see [1.4.3. Power analyses](#)<!--internal link formatting-->)_

- **Prepare a pre-analysis plan.**  
_Example: To strengthen your planned confirmatory analyses before collecting data, consider sharing a simulated dataset with a statistician or mentor. This allows for specific feedback on suitable statistical tests. The resulting analysis code can accompany your preregistration or registered report (see [1.4.1. Pre-analysis planning](<!--internal link-->)) so reviewers can clearly see your intended approach. When real data are collected, they can be directly substituted into the code to generate results._

<br>

Generating an artificial dataset in R (see our [simulation tutorial](https://lmu-osc.github.io/Introduction-Simulations-in-R/)) is much easier than researchers often believe it to be and is generally helpful, even when you need to make assumptions about variable distribution or when the parameter space is not well known.



::: {.container .my-4}

<h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE</h4>

::: {.row .row-cols-1 .row-cols-md-2 .row-cols-lg-3 .g-4 .mb-4}

<!-- card -->
::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-2">
<img src="https://www.r-project.org/logo/Rlogo.svg" class="img-fluid" alt="R logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">R Tutorial</h4>
<p class="card-text">Learn R programming. (3h)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/introduction-to-R/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"> </a>
</div>

:::

::: {.col}
<div class="card orc-card-tool w-100 h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Data simulation in R</h4>
<p class="card-text">Easy data simulations in R. (2h)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/Introduction-Simulations-in-R/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>
</div>

:::

:::

:::



#### 1.4.3. Power analyses 

Power analysis is relevant whether you are designing a project from scratch or running an analysis on already existing data. There are two main types of power analyses: 

<br>

##### A priori power analysis

**Simulate data to calculate the smallest sample size required to detect the smallest effect of interest.** See our [advanced tutorial](https://lmu-osc.github.io/Simulations-for-Advanced-Power-Analyses/). 

<br>

For a very basic power calculation, you can use simple R function if you know 3 out of 4 of these parameters: 

- required sample size n (usually the one missing)
- desired power (default 0.80)
- the alpha level (default 0.05)
- the expected effect size (has to be estimated or extracted from the literature on the form of d, f, etc.)

To get support with pre-analysis planning, you can book a consultation with the [LMU statistical consulting unit](mailto:@stablab.stat.uni-muenchen.de) and visit the [StaBLab website](https://www.stat.lmu.de/stablab/de/) for more information. 

<br>

##### Post-hoc power analysis

**Compute a post-hoc power when you are not be able to control the sample size for your project.** Beware: This power computation comes in two flavors - one is legitimate, and one is flawed and not defensible. 

<br>

The **legitimate post-hoc power** is computed with your actual n, and the same effect size that you plugged into your a-priori power analysis. This analysis gives you the achieved power to detect your assumed effect.

<br>

The **flawed version of post-hoc power** is called **“observed power”**: If an analysis yields a non-significant result, some researchers calculate the post-hoc power, but plug in the observed effect size. “Observed power”, however, is just a one‑to‑one function of the p‑value (a non-significant p-value returns a low power < 50 %, a just significant p-value of .05 always yields a power of exactly 50%). Observed power adds no new information to the p-value and is essentially meaningless. Do not compute this type of post-hoc power!


::: {.container .my-4}

<h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE</h4>

::: {.row .row-cols-1 .row-cols-md-2 .row-cols-lg-3 .g-4 .mb-4}

<!-- card -->
::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-2">
<img src="https://www.r-project.org/logo/Rlogo.svg" class="img-fluid" alt="R logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">R Tutorial</h4>
<p class="card-text">Learn R programming. (3h)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/introduction-to-R/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"> </a>
</div>

:::

::: {.col}
<div class="card orc-card-tool w-100 h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Power Analyses</h4>
<p class="card-text">Data simulations for GLMs, LMEs, and SEMs in R. (6h)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/Simulations-for-Advanced-Power-Analyses/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>
</div>

:::

:::

:::


:::


</details>

::: {.orc-gradient-box .plan-design-checklist }

## <i class="bi bi-card-checklist checklist-icon"></i> Plan & Design Checklist {.unnumbered}

To complete before presenting your final study plan to your research group and, if applicable, submitting your ethics proposal and/or preregistration. Not all items are relevant for all fields of research or study type.


::: {.row .row-cols-1 .row-cols-lg-2 .g-4}

::: {.col-md-6}


**Background Information**

- [ ] Literature reviewed 
- [ ] Existing research outputs searched
- [ ] Funder's Open Science requirements identified
- [ ] Ethics committee instructions consulted

**Study Design**

- [ ] Research questions clearly defined
- [ ] Hypotheses and predictions specified
- [ ] Study design chosen and justified
- [ ] Variables and measures to collect identified
- [ ] Analysis plan prepared (with simulated data)
- [ ] Sample size determined (with power analysis)
- [ ] Inclusion/exclusion criteria defined
- [ ] Handling of missing data defined

:::

::: {.col-md-6}

**Data Management Planning**

- [ ] Data storage and backup solutions documented
- [ ] File organization and naming conventions established
- [ ] Anonymization procedures defined
- [ ] Repository for data publishing selected

**Project Management**

- [ ] Team roles and responsibilities defined
- [ ] Resources needed for the project identified
- [ ] Timeline estimated and milestones defined

**Before Data Collection**

- [ ] Data Management Plan completed 
- [ ] Consent forms prepared
- [ ] Preregistration submitted 
- [ ] Ethical approval obtained 

:::

:::

::: {.d-flex .justify-content-center .mt-4 .orc-checklist-download .phase-1}

<a href="assets/checklists/Plan.Design.Checklist.docx" class="btn btn-outline-secondary btn-sm">Download checklist <i class="bi bi-download"></i></a>
<!-- need to update manually the  downloadable file with checklist in word doc + citation + licence in footnote + link to this webpage-->

:::


:::
